title: I recorded my grandmother before I knew why
summary: Narrate the impulse to record an elderly relative's voice and stories, and the unexpected weight of what would have been lost if the recording had never been made.
topic: Why is it worth recording an ordinary person's voice and stories before they are gone?
prompt: Write a 3-paragraph story (400–500 words) about deciding to record an elderly relative telling stories — almost on a whim — and only later understanding what that small act of preservation saved. Tell it as a narrative with specific moments, not as advice. Use the past simple and present perfect, at least one conditional, and 'although' or 'however' at least once.
hintStarters: I almost did not press record that afternoon, because it felt strange to point a phone at someone simply…
Although I had heard the story about the flood a hundred times and could have recited it myself, hearing it now in her own voice…
If I had waited until it felt important enough to do properly, I would have…

hintVocab.meaningVi: ghi âm giọng nói của ai đó
theo một ý thích bất chợt
một ký ức đang phai mờ
âm thanh của một giọng nói
một bản ghi âm không thể thay thế
giữ cho ký ức về ai đó còn sống

sampleText:
I almost did not press record that afternoon. My grandmother was halfway through a story I had heard many times — about the year the river flooded and her family slept on the roof for three nights — and it felt strange, almost rude, to point my phone at her as if she were a museum. But she was eighty-six, her voice had grown thin, and some quiet instinct told me to do it anyway. So I set the phone on the table between the teacups and let it run while she talked, and she did not even notice. The recording is forty minutes long. At the time it felt like nothing: just an old woman telling a story her grandson already knew by heart.

She died eighteen months later, more suddenly than any of us expected. In the first weeks I could not bring myself to listen to the file; it sat in my phone like a sealed letter I was not ready to open. When I finally did, on a train, I had to look out of the window so the other passengers would not see my face. It was not the story that undid me — I knew the story. It was the voice. The small breath she took before the funny part. The way she said my name. A particular phrase she used, half in her own dialect, that I had completely forgotten she said, and that I have not heard from any other living person since. Although the facts of the flood were never in danger of being lost, all of that — the texture of how she spoke — would have vanished the moment she did.

What shakes me, looking back, is how close I came to not doing it. If I had waited until it felt important enough to record her properly — a real microphone, a quiet room, a list of good questions — I would have waited forever, because that perfect occasion never arrives and then the person is gone. The clumsy phone on the tea table was the only version that was ever going to happen. I have since recorded my father telling the stories he is always telling, and I no longer feel strange doing it. We are taught to save photographs, but a photograph is silent. A voice carries the person in a way nothing else does, and it is astonishing how easily, and how permanently, we let it go. Press record. You will not regret the forty minutes. You may, one day, regret nothing so much as the recording you did not make.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for an absence continuing to the present
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'A particular phrase… that I have not heard from any other living person since' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành phủ định 'have not heard… since' để diễn tả rằng từ một thời điểm trong quá khứ cho đến nay, điều đó không hề xảy ra. Nó nhấn mạnh sự mất mát kéo dài đến hiện tại.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'A particular phrase… that I have not heard from any other living person since' uses the negative present perfect 'have not heard… since' to express that, from a past point until now, this has not happened. It stresses a loss that continues into the present.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Third conditional for a regret narrowly avoided
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If I had waited until it felt important enough…, I would have waited forever' dùng câu điều kiện loại 3 (if + past perfect, would have + V3) để hình dung một quá khứ KHÔNG xảy ra — sai lầm mà người kể đã suýt mắc phải. Nó làm tăng sức nặng cho việc người kể đã thực sự hành động.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If I had waited until it felt important enough…, I would have waited forever' uses the third conditional (if + past perfect, would have + past participle) to imagine a past that did NOT happen — the mistake the narrator nearly made. It adds weight to the action the narrator actually took.

sampleTranslationVi:
Tôi suýt đã không nhấn nút ghi âm chiều hôm đó. Bà tôi đang kể dở một câu chuyện tôi đã nghe nhiều lần — về cái năm con sông ngập lụt và gia đình bà ngủ trên mái nhà suốt ba đêm — và cảm giác thật lạ, gần như khiếm nhã, khi chĩa điện thoại vào bà như thể bà là một hiện vật bảo tàng. Nhưng bà đã tám mươi sáu tuổi, giọng bà đã trở nên yếu ớt, và một bản năng lặng lẽ nào đó bảo tôi cứ làm điều đó. Vậy là tôi đặt điện thoại lên bàn giữa những tách trà và để nó chạy trong khi bà nói, và bà thậm chí không để ý. Bản ghi dài bốn mươi phút. Lúc đó nó có vẻ chẳng là gì: chỉ là một bà lão kể một câu chuyện mà cháu trai của bà đã thuộc nằm lòng.

Bà mất mười tám tháng sau đó, đột ngột hơn bất kỳ ai trong chúng tôi ngờ tới. Trong những tuần đầu tôi không thể tự ép mình nghe tập tin đó; nó nằm trong điện thoại tôi như một lá thư niêm phong mà tôi chưa sẵn sàng mở. Khi cuối cùng tôi nghe, trên một chuyến tàu, tôi phải nhìn ra cửa sổ để những hành khách khác không thấy mặt mình. Không phải câu chuyện làm tôi suy sụp — tôi biết câu chuyện đó. Mà là giọng nói. Hơi thở nhỏ bà lấy trước đoạn vui. Cách bà gọi tên tôi. Một cách dùng từ riêng của bà, nửa bằng phương ngữ của bà, mà tôi đã quên hẳn là bà từng nói, và mà từ đó tôi chưa nghe được từ bất kỳ người sống nào khác. Mặc dù các tình tiết của trận lụt chưa bao giờ có nguy cơ bị mất, tất cả những điều đó — cái chất riêng trong cách bà nói — sẽ biến mất ngay khoảnh khắc bà ra đi.

Điều làm tôi rúng động, khi nhìn lại, là tôi đã suýt soát đến mức nào trong việc không làm điều đó. Nếu tôi đã chờ cho đến khi nó thấy đủ quan trọng để ghi âm bà một cách đàng hoàng — một chiếc micro thật, một căn phòng yên tĩnh, một danh sách những câu hỏi hay — tôi sẽ chờ mãi mãi, bởi vì cái dịp hoàn hảo đó không bao giờ đến và rồi người đó đã ra đi. Chiếc điện thoại vụng về trên bàn trà là phiên bản duy nhất từng có thể xảy ra. Kể từ đó tôi đã ghi âm bố tôi kể những câu chuyện mà ông luôn kể, và tôi không còn thấy lạ khi làm điều đó nữa. Chúng ta được dạy phải giữ những bức ảnh, nhưng một bức ảnh thì im lặng. Một giọng nói mang theo con người ấy theo cách mà không gì khác làm được, và thật đáng kinh ngạc là chúng ta để nó tuột đi dễ dàng đến thế, và vĩnh viễn đến thế. Hãy nhấn nút ghi âm. Bạn sẽ không hối tiếc bốn mươi phút đó. Một ngày nào đó, có thể bạn sẽ không hối tiếc điều gì nhiều bằng bản ghi âm mà bạn đã không thực hiện.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the negative present perfect with 'since' for a loss continuing to now?
options: It is a phrase I have not hear from anyone since.
It is a phrase I did not hear from anyone since.
It is a phrase I have not heard from anyone ago.
It is a phrase I have not heard from anyone since.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Have not heard ... since' is the correct present perfect for an absence up to now. Option 2 uses the base verb 'hear'. Option 3 uses past simple, which does not connect to now with 'since'. Option 4 misuses 'ago', which needs a past simple and a time length.
mcQuestions.hint: Present perfect = have + past participle ('heard'); pair it with 'since', not 'ago'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the third conditional correctly to describe a mistake nearly made?
options: If I had waited for the perfect moment, I would have waited forever.
If I had waited for the perfect moment, I would waited forever.
If I would have waited for the perfect moment, I would have waited forever.
If I waited for the perfect moment, I would have waited forever.
mcQuestions.explanation: Third conditional = if + past perfect, would have + past participle. Option 2 drops 'have'. Option 3 puts 'would have' in the if-clause. Option 4 uses past simple in the if-clause.
mcQuestions.hint: Unreal past: if + had + participle, then would have + participle.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'although' correctly to concede that the facts were safe?
options: Although the facts were never in danger of being lost, but the voice itself was.
Although the facts were never in danger of being lost, the voice itself was.
Although the facts were never in danger of being lost the voice itself was.
The facts were never in danger of being lost, although however the voice itself was.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, + main clause' is correct. Option 2 adds 'but'. Option 3 omits the comma. Option 4 stacks 'although' and 'however'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Although' needs a comma and stands alone, without 'but' or 'however'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option most naturally describes acting on a sudden, unplanned impulse?
options: I recorded it on a wish.
I recorded it in a whim.
I recorded it on a whim.
I recorded it by a whim.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'On a whim' is the fixed idiom for a sudden impulse. 'On a wish' is not idiomatic. 'In a whim' and 'by a whim' use the wrong preposition.
mcQuestions.hint: The set phrase uses 'on' plus a word meaning a sudden urge.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'know something by heart'?
options: Her grandson already knew the story by the heart.
Her grandson already knew the story from heart.
Her grandson already knew the story in heart.
Her grandson already knew the story by heart.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Know something by heart' (from memory) is the fixed idiom. Option 2 adds 'the'. Option 3 uses 'from'. Option 4 uses 'in'.
mcQuestions.hint: The idiom for memorising is 'by heart', with no article.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'come close to + gerund'?
options: It shakes me how close I came to not doing it.
It shakes me how close I came to not do it.
It shakes me how close I came not doing it.
It shakes me how close I came to not did it.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Come close to + gerund' is correct ('to not doing'). Option 2 uses the base verb 'do'. Option 3 drops 'to'. Option 4 uses the past 'did' after 'to'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Close to' is followed by the '-ing' form.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence is the most natural way to describe the recording's effect?
options: It was the voice what undid me.
It was the voice that undid me.
It was the voice that undone me.
It was the voice which undid me to pieces.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It was the voice that undid me' is a correct cleft sentence with 'that' and the past 'undid'. Option 2 uses 'what' instead of 'that'. Option 3 uses the participle 'undone' instead of the past 'undid'. Option 4 adds the awkward 'to pieces'.
mcQuestions.hint: Cleft sentences use 'that'; the past simple of 'undo' is 'undid'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence most naturally contrasts a photo with a recording?
options: We are taught saving photographs, but a photograph is silent.
We are taught to save photographs, but a photograph is silence.
We are taught to save photographs, but a photograph is silent.
We are taught to saving photographs, but a photograph is silent.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Taught to save' uses the infinitive correctly, and 'silent' is the adjective needed. Option 2 uses 'saving' after 'taught'. Option 3 uses the noun 'silence' where an adjective is needed. Option 4 says 'to saving'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Taught to + verb' takes the infinitive; describe the photo with the adjective 'silent'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'in a way + clause' to praise the voice?
options: A voice carries the person in a way nothing else do.
A voice carries the person in a way how nothing else does.
A voice carries the person in the way nothing else doing.
A voice carries the person in a way nothing else does.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'In a way + clause' with 'does' agreeing with 'nothing else' is correct. Option 2 uses 'do' without agreement. Option 3 adds a redundant 'how'. Option 4 uses 'doing' and the wrong article.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Nothing else' takes the singular 'does'; no 'how' is needed after 'way'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best states the story's closing warning clearly and naturally?
options: You may one day regret nothing so much as the recording you did not make.
You may one day regret nothing so much than the recording you did not make.
You may one day regret nothing as much like the recording you did not make.
You may one day regret nothing so much as the recording you did not made.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Nothing so much as' is the correct comparative idiom, with 'did not make' (base verb after 'did not'). Option 2 uses 'than' instead of 'as'. Option 3 uses 'as much like'. Option 4 uses 'did not made'.
mcQuestions.hint: The idiom is 'so much as'; after 'did not', use the base verb 'make'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The narrator realises that waiting for the 'perfect occasion' would have meant never recording at all. Is there someone whose voice or stories you would regret losing? Why do you think we so often postpone simple acts of preservation until it is too late to do them?
